title: On the Realization of Some Degenerate Automorphic Forms on Certain Griffiths-Schmid Varieties
speaker: Henri Carayol
abstract: GALOIS REPRESENTATIONS AND AUTOMORPHIC FORMS SEMINARSome automorphic forms, despite the fact they are algebraic, do not have any interpretation as cohomology classes on a Shimura variety: therefore nothing is known at present on their expected arithmetic properties. I shall explain how such forms appear to be related to more general objects (Griffiths-Schmid varieties) and discuss some related rationality questions.

proof_stages.label: Galois and Maass-form motivation for degenerate automorphic forms
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the contrast between odd two-dimensional Galois representations coming from weight-one modular forms and mysterious even representations expected to correspond to Maass forms, motivating passage via symmetric square functoriality to automorphic representations on U(2,1).

proof_stages.label: Harish-Chandra limits and the U(2,1) degenerate point
proof_stages.summary: Harish-Chandra's parametrization of discrete series and their limits is recalled, and the U(2,1) root diagram is used to identify the degenerate limit of discrete series that will be the target representation.

proof_stages.label: Replacement of Shimura varieties by Griffiths-Schmid varieties
proof_stages.summary: Because degenerate limits do not occur in ordinary Shimura-variety coherent cohomology, Griffiths-Schmid varieties are introduced and, for U(2,1), the relevant flag-domain orbit Omega is separated from the two Picard-modular-surface-type orbits X and Y.

proof_stages.label: Cohomology computation locating the degenerate limit on Omega
proof_stages.summary: Using line bundles attached to torus characters and relative Lie-algebra cohomology, the cohomology of the compact quotient of Omega is computed and the degenerate limit is located in degrees one and two.

proof_stages.label: Cup-product interaction with holomorphic and antiholomorphic forms
proof_stages.summary: The degenerate classes on Omega are shown to interact nontrivially, up to Hecke translates, with the nearest holomorphic and antiholomorphic discrete-series classes via cup products, giving generators in the relevant H^2.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological correspondences among X, Y, and Omega
proof_stages.summary: Degree-shifting maps between cohomology spaces on the three flag-domain quotients are constructed by explicit Dolbeault formulas, are shown to be Hecke-compatible and isomorphisms on discrete-series parts, and are proposed as the mechanism for transferring rational structures.

proof_stages.label: Boundary Fourier-Jacobi comparison and rationality
proof_stages.summary: For noncompact quotients, Fourier-Jacobi expansions along boundary elliptic curves are compared with analogous H^1-valued coefficients on the partial compactification of Omega, and the resulting composite isomorphism is rational.

proof_stages.label: Shimura-curve restriction and period obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Restriction to horizontal Shimura curves is compared with the corresponding restriction on the dual curve, but the Petersson-pairing/Serre-duality comparison introduces periods, so this curve test does not give the same rationality as the boundary expansion and appears inadequate for non-holomorphic types.

examples.name: Weight-one modular forms and even Galois representations
examples.note: The classical weight-one modular-form/Galois-representation story supplies the motivating GL2 contrast that leads to seeking a better U(2,1) realization, although the later construction does not depend on this example.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses weight-one modular forms and even two-dimensional Galois representations only as motivating instances, stating their known or conjectural correspondences without carrying out a concrete computation or construction on a specific form/representation.

passes.reason: Weight-one modular forms are the lowest-weight boundary/limit-of-discrete-series case, while the even Galois representations are introduced as a motivating missing parity case rather than as a refuting example.
Weight-one modular forms are used as a motivating boundary/limit-of-discrete-series case, while even Galois representations mark the missing Langlands counterpart rather than a counterexample.
Weight-one forms are a minimal limit-of-discrete-series boundary case, while even Galois representations are presented as the parity cases not realized by the usual odd weight-one construction.
Weight-one forms are a minimal/boundary limit-of-discrete-series case, while the even Galois representations are used as motivating missing counterparts rather than to refute a claim.

Weight one is the lowest/boundary holomorphic modular-form case with archimedean component a limit of discrete series, while even Galois representations are motivational targets rather than refutations.
Weight-one forms are a minimal/boundary limit-of-discrete-series case motivating the even Galois/Maass correspondence, not used to refute a claim.
Weight-one modular forms are used as motivating low-weight boundary/nondegenerate limit cases leading to the even-Galois-representation question, not as a refutation.

Weight-one modular forms are used as a motivating boundary case: their Archimedean component is a nondegenerate limit of discrete series, while even Galois representations motivate passing to Maass/degenerate settings rather than serving as a direct counterexample.
Weight-one modular forms are used as motivating boundary/limit-of-discrete-series cases for the odd/even Galois-representation question, not to refute a claim.
Weight one is the boundary/minimal holomorphic modular weight, while even Galois representations are invoked as cases not covered by the odd Deligne–Serre weight-one correspondence.
Weight-one modular forms are a minimal/boundary-weight case, used here as motivation for the missing even Galois-representation side rather than as a counterexample.

Even two-dimensional Galois representations are invoked as the cases not accounted for by classical weight-one modular forms, while weight one is the boundary/minimal holomorphic weight corresponding to a limit of discrete series.
